title: Coffee on Carpet
stainChemistry: Coffee contains tannins (polyphenolic compounds from roasted beans) that bond to carpet through hydrogen bonding and pi-stacking interactions. Fresh coffee is water-soluble. Aged stains oxidize and darken — the brown color intensifies as tannin molecules cross-link. Melanoidins from the roasting process add additional brown pigment that is highly resistant to removal.
whyThisWorks: An acidic tannin spotter (professional) or oxidizer (DIY) breaks the chromophore bonds — the colored structures — in the tannin molecules on carpet. Hydrogen peroxide specifically targets the double bonds that make oxidized tannin brown. The key is avoiding heat and alkaline products, both of which permanently darken tannin stains.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color
Avoid over-wetting — extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test on a hidden spot
homeSolutions.instruction: Find a hidden patch of the same carpet — inside a closet, under furniture, behind a door. Dab a drop of the Dawn + 3% peroxide solution from step 5 and blot with a white cloth after two minutes. If the carpet holds its color, you're safe to treat. If dye transfers or the fiber lightens, skip to step 9 and bring in a pro — home peroxide will lift the dye.

homeSolutions.agent: The tannin rule: no alkaline cleaners
homeSolutions.instruction: Coffee is a tannin, just like wine, tea, and berries. Alkaline products — ammonia, baking soda, OxiClean, most "all-purpose" carpet sprays and foams — lock tannin into the carpet fiber permanently and turn a light brown ring into a dark, unreverseable one. Use only: cold water, Dawn dish soap, 3% hydrogen peroxide, and white vinegar. Nothing else.

Press a clean white cloth or stack of paper towels straight down on the spill. Lift, move to a dry section, press again. Work from the outside of the stain inward so you don't spread it. Keep blotting until the cloth stops picking up brown. Rubbing drives coffee deep into the pile and the pad below — and pad contamination is how you end up with a permanent ring.

Pour a small amount of cold water directly on the stain — a few tablespoons, not a cup — and blot immediately. Repeat two or three times. Fresh coffee is water-soluble, so early dilution pulls most of the stain out before the real chemistry starts. Avoid hot water, even if the spill was hot: hot water drives tannin deeper into the fiber and triggers browning.

Mix one tablespoon of clear Dawn dish soap with one cup of 3% hydrogen peroxide in a small bowl. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, and dab the stain. Let it sit 5–10 minutes. Peroxide oxidizes the tannin color molecules; Dawn lifts them. Blot with a clean damp cloth. Most coffee stains lift in 1–2 cycles; older, heavier stains may need 3.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix hydrogen peroxide with ammonia or bleach. On dark or patterned carpet, do not apply this solution unless the patch test came back clean.

homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one tablespoon of white vinegar into one cup of cold water. Dab onto the treated spot with a clean white cloth. The mild acid neutralizes any peroxide residue and keeps the tannin in its lighter form as it dries. Blot dry with a fresh clean cloth.

homeSolutions.agent: Dry down — prevent pad wicking overnight
homeSolutions.instruction: Blot until no moisture transfers to a dry cloth. Stack six to ten sheets of white paper towels over the spot, weigh them down with a heavy book, and leave overnight. This pulls any residual coffee up out of the carpet pad before it can wick back to the surface as a ring. Don't walk on the area or vacuum until completely dry.

homeSolutions.agent: Check under daylight — repeat if shadow remains
homeSolutions.instruction: Once dry, inspect in natural light, not a lamp. Coffee rings often look clean when wet and reappear as a pale tan halo when dry. If you see one, repeat steps 5–7 a second time. Two rounds is the home ceiling. If a shadow still shows after two full rounds, the tannin has set and a pro will be faster than a third DIY pass.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a professional carpet cleaner
homeSolutions.instruction: If the patch test fails, the carpet is wool, or the stain survives two full rounds, call a pro. Tell them: "Coffee on [nylon/polyester/wool] carpet, treated with Dawn + 3% peroxide, vinegar rinse. No ammonia or alkaline products used. Residual tan halo remains." That handoff lets them jump straight to a tannin formula + 6% peroxide + truck-mount extraction — the full tool chain you can't replicate at home.

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No chlorine bleach on colored carpet
Avoid over-wetting — causes backing damage and mold
fiberSensitivities: Most synthetic carpet (nylon/polyester) tolerates standard chemistry
Always blot from outside in to prevent spreading
Extract excess moisture to prevent backing damage

escalation.when: If coffee stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is coffee on carpet, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaning service with truck-mount extraction

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
